Core.description: Portable application, request, data, configuration, error, and security baseline.

Standard.description: Core plus identity, durable background work, observability, API, and shape evidence.

Complete.description: Standard plus rendering, realtime, notifications, and deployment evidence.

resultSemantics.pass: All assertions ran and matched the normalized expected result.
resultSemantics.fail: At least one assertion ran and observed a protocol mismatch.
resultSemantics.skipped: The runner recognizes the requirement but did not execute it because a declared prerequisite was unavailable.
resultSemantics.unsupported: The implementation declares that the required capability is unavailable.
resultSemantics.exception: A public RFC grants a time-bounded deviation; an exception never satisfies a profile requirement.
resultSemantics.experimental: Evidence was collected for an unstable capability; an experimental result never satisfies a profile or extension badge.

requirements.statement: Canonical application roles map to documented paths or language-idiomatic equivalents.
User-owned application code takes precedence over framework defaults at the same logical path.
Discovery and registration rules are deterministic and documented, including negative cases.
Naming rules for routes, tables, keys, environment variables, jobs, and generated artifacts are deterministic.
A scaffolded application starts in documented local-development mode after declared prerequisites are installed.
An Action is invokable independently of HTTP or another exposing transport.
A Model depends on neither an HTTP request nor a UI renderer.
Generated migrations are reviewable before application and identify destructive changes.
Generated artifacts are deterministic for identical input and tool versions.
Generated artifacts identify their source revision and generator version or an equivalent provenance manifest.
Machine-readable adapters return stable error envelopes containing category or code, message, status, and optional field errors.
Production errors expose stack traces, credentials, raw queries, or filesystem paths.
Method and path matching, parameters, groups, middleware attachment, and named routes behave deterministically.
Named routes reverse-generate URLs with validated required parameters.
Observable request execution preserves middleware, validation, authorization, Action, serialization or rendering, and response order.
An Action executes after middleware, validation, or authorization produces a terminal response.
Validation produces stable field-keyed errors for nested invalid input.
Validation rejects invalid input before Action execution and documents any coercion.
At least one persistence adapter supports CRUD, filtering, ordering, and pagination.
Transactions roll back all writes when the transactional operation fails.
Supported relationship and eager-loading behavior is reported per tested database dialect.
Migrations apply in deterministic order and are reversible or explicitly compensating.
Destructive migration operations are visible in a plan or preview before production application.
Defaults, files, environment overrides, and explicit overrides have deterministic documented precedence.
Invalid selected capabilities and missing required credentials fail clearly before unsafe work begins.
Secrets are embedded in generated client bundles.
Database operations parameterize untrusted values by default.
Rendered untrusted values are escaped by default.
Applicable browser-authenticated writes reject missing or invalid CSRF tokens.
Security headers are configurable and documented.
Application encryption helpers provide authenticated encryption and reject modified ciphertext.
Passwords use an algorithm intended for password storage and configurable work factors.
Authenticator comparison is timing-safe where applicable.
Trust boundaries, storage assumptions, key lifecycle, and security non-guarantees are documented.
Identity establishment, authorization gates, token or session revocation, and storage assumptions are tested.
At least one non-inline queue driver persists and executes named serialized Jobs with retries, timeouts, and failure recording.
Unsupported or planned drivers fail loudly without fallback or silent degradation.
Each supported driver is reported separately with runtime, platform, topology, provider version, and evidence revision.
A request or trace identifier propagates through structured logs into at least one background-work path.
Liveness and readiness surfaces distinguish process health from configured dependency readiness.
The implementation publishes checked shape evidence and identifies inferred, generated, cast, and runtime-only boundaries.
A machine-readable API adapter applies validation, stable serialization, status codes, and the protocol error envelope.
A rendered-view adapter projects prepared data while preserving output escaping and authorization boundaries.
Machine-readable descriptions generated from registered routes and schemas are fresh and non-placeholder.
Bidirectional events support public and authorization-gated channels, bounded payloads, rate limits, and documented scale-out behavior.
A notification abstraction has at least two tested channels and isolates credentials and delivery failures from domain logic.
At least one deployment adapter produces a reviewable plan and evidence for its tested provider and topology.

compatibility.directoryMappings: Mappings may be language-idiomatic but changing resolution precedence is behavioral and requires migration guidance.
compatibility.errorEnvelopes: Adding optional fields is compatible only when consumers ignore unknown fields; changing required fields, categories, or status mapping is breaking.
compatibility.driverContracts: Adding a driver is compatible; changing capability semantics or silent fallback behavior is behavioral or breaking.
compatibility.generatedSchemas: Additive optional schema changes may be compatible; required-field, type, or meaning changes require a protocol-version decision.
